question: Selling goods and services on credit is:

options.A: an investment in a customer.
options.B: never necessary unless customers cannot pay for the goods.
options.C: a decision independent of customers.
options.D: permissible only if your bank lends the money.
options.E: never a wise decision.

question: When credit is granted by one firm to another firm this gives rise to a(n):

options.A: accounts receivable and is called consumer credit.
options.B: credit due and is called an installment note.
options.C: accounts receivable and is called trade credit.
options.D: trade receivable and is called an installment note.
options.E: trade receivable and is called a secured loan.

question: The average collection period measures the average:

options.A: time necessary to collect a credit sale.
options.B: number of customers per day who charge their purchases.
options.C: time for a credit customer to return to make a second purchase.
options.D: number of times a credit customer charges a purchase during a year.
options.E: number of items purchased in each credit transaction.

question: The three components of credit policy are:

options.A: collection policy, credit analysis, and interest rate determination.
options.B: collection policy, credit analysis, and terms of the sale.
options.C: collection policy, interest rate determination, and repayment analysis.
options.D: credit analysis, repayment analysis, and terms of the sale.
options.E: interest rate determination, repayment analysis, and terms of sale.

question: Credit analysis is best described as the process of:

options.A: collecting an accounts receivable.
options.C: establishing the length of the credit period.
options.D: setting the amount of discount to be granted.
options.E: determining the probability that a customer will not pay.

question: Seasonal dating is used to promote sales during the off-season. This process involves:

options.A: extending the credit period until after the season ends.
options.B: extending both the discount period and the credit period by two months.
options.C: accepting orders early but withholding shipment until the peak season.
options.D: accepting orders early but dating the invoice when the goods are actually shipped.
options.E: dating an invoice at a later date than when the goods are shipped.

question: Credit terms of 1/5, net 15 should be interpreted as granting:

options.A: a 1/5 percent discount for payments within 15 days.
options.B: a five percent discount for next day payments.
options.C: a total credit period of 20 days.
options.D: a credit period of 10 days.
options.E: a one percent discount for payments received within five days.

question: The credit period begins on the:

options.A: shipping date.
options.C: shipping arrival date.
options.D: order process date.
options.E: invoice date.

question: On September 1, a firm grants credit with terms of 2/10 net 30. The creditor:

options.A: must pay a penalty of 2/10 of one percent when payment is made later than October 1.
options.B: must pay a penalty of 10 percent when payment is made later than 2 days after October 1.
options.C: receives a discount of 2 percent when payment is made at least 10 days prior to October 1.
options.D: receives a discount of 2 percent when payment is made on September 1 and pays a penalty of 10 percent if payment is made after October 1.
options.E: receives a discount of 2 percent when payment is made within 10 days.

question: The length of the credit period offered by a firm is influenced by all of the following except the:

options.A: level of consumer demand.
options.C: standardization of the goods being sold.
options.D: FTC guidelines for trade credit.
options.E: customer type.

question: Seasonal dating of accounts receivable:

options.A: is used by all firms that grant credit.
options.B: sets the first date of the relevant season as the final due date for an invoice for seasonal goods.
options.C: sets a relevant seasonal date as the invoice date for an earlier order.
options.D: refers to firms that invoice every quarter for sales made in the prior three months.
options.E: requires all purchasers of seasonal goods to have their purchases paid by the end of the prior season.

question: Which one of the following statements is false as it relates to considerations firms use when establishing a credit policy?

options.A: A firm that supplies a perishable product will tend to offer restrictive credit terms.
options.B: A firm whose customers are in a high-risk business will tend to offer restrictive credit terms.
options.C: Lengthening the credit period effectively reduces the price paid by the customer.
options.D: Small accounts, associated with firms that find it difficult to acquire a line of credit, tend to receive longer credit periods.
options.E: Larger accounts tend to receive more favorable credit terms.

question: The upper limit to the credit period is best expressed as the length of the:

options.A: seller's operating cycle.
options.C: seller's payables period.
options.D: buyer's operating cycle.
options.E: buyer's cash cycle.

question: Cash discounts:

options.A: increase the amount of credit offered.
options.C: speed up the collection of receivables.
options.D: were first offered in the early 1900s.
options.E: are a cost-free means of increasing sales.

question: With an open account the formal instrument of credit is the:

options.A: purchase order.
options.C: promissory note.
options.D: banker's acceptance.
options.E: secured loan document.

question: Which one of these statements is true regarding promissory notes?

options.A: Most trade credit arrangements use promissory notes.
options.B: Promissory notes are used when firms do not anticipate a problem with collections.
options.C: Promissory notes usually involve no cash discount.
options.D: A promissory note must be signed and delivered prior to goods being shipped.
options.E: Promissory notes are used for small orders only.

question: A commercial draft typically:

options.A: specifies the payment amount and payment due date.
options.B: specifies that the purchaser use the seller's bank as the guarantor.
options.C: requires payment prior to the delivery of the goods.
options.D: is signed upon delivery of the goods.
options.E: involves a lien on the purchasers' current assets.

question: Which one of the following statements is false?

options.A: Commercial drafts represent a way to obtain a credit commitment from a customer before the goods are delivered.
options.B: When a banker's acceptance is discounted in the secondary market it becomes a commercial note.
options.C: Sight drafts require immediate payment.
options.D: Banker's acceptances arise when a bank guarantees payment on a commercial draft.
options.E: A commercial draft becomes a trade acceptance once the buyer accepts the draft and promises to pay.

question: One characteristic of a conditional sales contract is that the:

options.A: seller retains legal ownership until the buyer completes payment for the goods.
options.B: buyer is compensated for its opportunity costs.
options.C: seller receives a prepayment in full.
options.D: invoice is paid in one lump sum at the end of the credit period.
options.E: ownership of the goods changes to the buyer immediately upon delivery.

question: The net credit period for a company with terms of 2/10, net 45 is:

options.A: 10 days.
options.C: 35 days.
options.D: 55 days.
options.E: 40 days.

question: The decision to grant credit should consider all the following except the:

options.A: delay in revenues from granting credit.
options.C: probability of nonpayment.
options.D: cost of short-term borrowing.
options.E: fixed costs incurred during the credit period.

question: When analyzing the NPV of a decision to switch from a cash-only sales policy to a credit policy with an early payment discount, the firm is least apt to consider the:

options.A: size of the discount.
options.C: firm's variable costs.
options.D: expected change in sales.
options.E: fixed salaries of the sales force.

question: When analyzing the decision to change the cash discount policy, the firm should select the policy that has the:

options.A: highest order size.
options.C: lowest NPV.
options.D: highest NPV.
options.E: lowest cash discount.

question: Assume you graph the costs of granting credit against the amount of credit extended. The optimal credit amount is then determined by the point which:

options.A: minimizes the total cost curve.
options.B: maximizes the carrying costs associated with granting credit.
options.C: maximizes the opportunity costs associated with granting credit.
options.D: minimizes the carrying costs of granting credit.
options.E: minimizes the opportunity costs of granting credit.

question: Determining the optimal credit policy is based on a trade-off between the carrying costs of granting credit and the:

options.A: lost profits from refusing credit.
options.B: cash flows delayed from granting credit.
options.C: opportunity cost of the delayed payments.
options.D: variable costs associated with the delayed payments.
options.E: present value of uncollected sales.

question: One key reason for establishing a captive finance company is the:

options.A: reduction of legal restrictions on the amount of debt that can be incurred.
options.C: lower level of required financial insurance.
options.D: anticipated decrease in accounts receivable.
options.E: expected decrease in the cost of the debt required to finance receivables.

question: All the following can provide credit information about a customer except:

options.A: the customer's financial statements.
options.C: the customer's current payment history with the seller.
options.D: the amount of goods the customer desires to purchase.
options.E: banks.

question: Since the credit decision usually includes riskier customers, the decision should adjust for this by:

options.A: determining the probability of nonpayment and reducing the expected cash flows accordingly.
options.B: discounting the net cash flows at a lower discount rate.
options.C: discounting the cash inflows at a higher discount rate.
options.D: increasing the variable cost per unit.
options.E: decreasing the variable cost per unit.

question: All the following are one of the "Five C's of Credit" except:

options.A: capability.
options.C: capital.
options.D: character.
options.E: conditions.

question: Which one of the following statements is false?

options.A: An aging schedule includes only overdue accounts.
options.B: Aging schedules are used to monitor accounts receivable.
options.C: If sales are seasonal, the percentages shown on an aging schedule will vary during the year.
options.D: Collection efforts may involve legal action.
options.E: Investments in accounts receivable equal average daily sales times average collection period.

question: To collect on the accounts receivable due to the firm, a firm can do all the following except:

options.A: send a delinquency letter of past due status to the customer.
options.C: employ a collection agency.
options.D: take legal action against the customer as necessary.
options.E: forcibly remove property from the buyer's premises.

question: Windshield glass purchased by an automaker and sitting on a shelf ready for use is classified as:

options.A: finished goods inventory.
options.C: assembly materials.
options.D: work-in-progress.
options.E: partial-goods inventory.

question: Which one of these statements is correct?

options.A: Finished goods are classified as a commodity.
options.B: Work-in-progress may have less resell value than the individual component parts did.
options.C: Raw materials that are considered to be a commodity are generally illiquid.
options.D: Raw materials consist of only those goods that are found in nature.
options.E: Finished goods are highly liquid because they are completed.

question: All of these are carrying costs of inventory except:

options.A: storage costs.
options.C: restocking costs.
options.D: theft.
options.E: the opportunity cost of capital.

question: The basic assumption of the ABC approach to inventory management is that:

options.C: firms should receive A customer's order Before incurring inventory Costs.

options.A: inventory is held to its daily minimum level.
options.B: inventory is maintained at a level equal to one week's production needs.

options.B: Order size × Variable cost per unit.

question: For a JIT inventory system to be efficient, the:

options.C: managers must limit production each day to a set quantity.

question: Crocket Motors has an account receivable balance of $682,400 and the average collection period is 38 days. What are the firm's credit sales per day?
explanation: Daily credit sales = Accounts receivable/Average collection period Daily credit sales = $682,400/38 Daily credit sales = $17,957.89

question: Delta Distributors has accounts receivable of $2,750,000 and average daily credit sales of $118,280. The firm offers credit terms of 2/10, net 30. On average, what is the firm's accounts receivable period?

options.A: 19.47 days
options.C: 37.14 days
options.D: 20.00 days
options.E: 18.64 days

explanation: Accounts receivable period = Accounts receivable/Average daily sales Accounts receivable period = $2,750,000/$118,280 Accounts receivable period = 23.25 days

question: If 34 percent of customers pay on Day 10 and the remainder pay in an average of 28 days, what is the average collection period?

options.A: 19.72 days
options.C: 21.88 days
options.D: 18.47 days
options.E: 22.09 days

question: Lemoyne mailed an invoice today in the amount of $1,268 with terms of 2/7 net 30. What is the cost of credit to the customer if they pay on the last day of the credit period? Assume a 365- day year.

options.A: 41.02 percent
options.C: 37.80 percent
options.D: 37.56 percent
options.E: 39.40 percent

question: Jaxon Markets currently has credit terms of net 30, an average collection period of 29 days, and average receivables of $211,410. The firm estimates that if it offered terms of 2/10, net 30 that 45 percent of its customers would pay on Day 10 with the remainder paying on average in 32 days. How much cash could the company free up from its accounts receivables if it switched its credit policy?
explanation: Average daily sales = Accounts receivable/Average collection period Average daily sales = $211,410/29 Average daily sales = $7,290. New average collection period = .45(10) + .55(32) New average collection period = 22.10 days New accounts receivable = Average daily sales (Average collection period) New accounts receivable = $7,290(22.10) New accounts receivable = $161,109 Cash freed up = Old accounts receivable − New accounts receivable Cash freed up = $211,410 − 161,109 Cash freed up = $50,301

question: Yesterday, Smiley Company sold $22,500 of merchandise on credit. The invoice was sent today with the terms, 3/10 net 40. This customer normally pays on the net date. What is the effective rate of interest the customer is paying by not taking the discount? Assume a 365-day year.

options.A: 42.31 percent
options.C: 39.27 percent
options.D: 40.54 percent
options.E: 45.38 percent

question: Edgeworth Co. has an all-cash policy and sells 50 units per month at $920 a unit. The variable cost is $700 a unit. Should the firm grant 30 days of credit, it expects its sales would rise to 60 units without changes to price or costs per unit. The monthly required return is .75 percent. What is the NPV of switching to a credit policy?

Assume Atlantic Fish sells 3,200 pounds of fish per month at a price of $2.90 a pound. The variable cost per pound is $2.22. Currently, the firm has a cash-only sales policy. The firm is considering changing to a net 30 credit policy. The monthly required return is 1.2 percent. What does the new level of sales need to be to break even on the switch?

options.A: 3,219.40 pounds
options.C: 3,370.44 pounds
options.D: 170.44 pounds
options.E: 119.40 pounds

question: Lewis Companies sells 2,600 units a month for cash at a price of $299 a unit and a variable cost of $187 a unit. The firm estimates it can increase its sales by 200 units a month if it switches to a net 30 credit policy while keeping its price and costs at their current levels. If the monthly cost of capital is .85 percent, what is the NPV of switching?

Underwood United has been approached by a new customer who has asked the firm to extend credit for 30 days on a one-time purchase of $499. The firm's required return on receivables is 1.8 percent per month and the variable cost of the desired item is $327. What is the NPV of granting credit if the firm estimates the probability of default is 15 percent?

Alexander Moore & Co. is willing to offer credit on a one-time purchase provided the NPV of the transaction is at least $50 at a required monthly return of 2 percent. Assume a potential sale has a sales price of $248 and a variable cost of $164. What is the maximum probability of default that will result in an acceptable offer?

options.A: 32.55 percent
options.C: 11.98 percent
options.D: 10.02 percent
options.E: 18.50 percent

question: A&M Hardware assumes new customers will default 8 percent of the time but if they don't default, they will become repeat customers who always pay their bills. Assume the average sale is $383 with a variable cost of $260, and a monthly required return of 1.65 percent. What is the NPV of extending credit for one month to a new customer?

Neilson's is a new firm that sells a product with a variable cost of $62 a unit. The firm has a monthly required return of 1.8 percent. The firm wants to offer all new customers 30 days of credit and expects that if it does so, that 12 percent will default on payment while the others become repeat customers. What is the minimum price the firm could charge to break-even on an NPV basis?
Sisler's sells 382,000 units a year and orders 10,000 units at a time. The cost of placing an order is $24.90. What is the firm's annual total restocking cost?
explanation: Total restocking cost = Fixed cost per order (Number of orders) Total restocking cost = $24.90(382,000/10,000) Total restocking cost = $951.18

question: Jensen's Boat Works total costs of holding inventory is $8,400 when its order sizes are optimized. If the firm places 46 orders a year, what is the fixed cost per order?
explanation: At the optimal order size, carrying costs equal restocking costs. Thus, restocking costs equal 50 percent of the total costs of holding inventory. Total restocking cost = .50(Total costs) = Fixed cost per order (Number of orders) Fixed cost per order = .50($8,400)/46 Fixed cost per order = $91.30

question: Fried Onions has total annual sales of 438,000 units, a carrying cost per unit of $2.67 per year, and restocking costs of $48 per order. What is the EOQ?

options.A: 4,203 units
options.C: 3,968 units
options.D: 4,126 units
options.E: 4,511 units

question: Baked Potatoes has total annual sales of 846,000 units, a carrying cost per unit of $1.64 per year, and restocking costs of $31 per order. Each inventory item has an average cost of $2.39. What is the average dollar value of the firm's inventory if it always orders the most economical quantity?
explanation: Q* = (2TF/CC).5 Q* = [2(846,000)($31)/$1.64].5 Q* = 5,655 units Average inventory value = (Q*/2)(Cost per unit) Average inventory value = (5,655/2)($2.39) Average inventory value = $6,758

question: Identify several factors that affect the length of the credit period and provide an explanation of each. Answer: The length of the credit period is affected by: ∙ Perishability and collateral value: The credit period should be less than the life of the item being financed. For example, if bananas have a shelf life of one-week then the credit period should probably be one week or less. ∙ Consumer demand: The credit period should be inversely related to consumer demand. In other words, the higher the demand, the shorter the credit period. ∙ Cost, profitability, and standardization: The greater the standardization and the lower the cost, generally the shorter the credit period. ∙ Credit risk: As with any risk, the less exposure to it the better. Thus, the higher the risk, the shorter the credit period. ∙ Size of the account: The larger the account size the longer the credit period that may be granted, especially if an account represents a significant portion of a firm's sales. ∙ Competition: In a competitive market, credit periods may be extended as a means of gaining sales. ∙ Customer type: The type of customer and their operating cycle will influence the credit period. Some sellers are willing to finance a buyer's inventory period and maybe even its accounts receivable period, but not beyond. Difficulty: 2 Medium Section: 28.2 Terms of the Sale Topic: Credit terms Bloom's: Apply AACSB: Knowledge Application Accessibility: Keyboard Navigation

Green Garden is a cash-only company. The company is considering switching to a 30-day credit policy with no discounts. What factors should the firm consider before making the switch? Answer: The firm should consider the price it can charge for credit sales, any anticipated changes in sales quantity, and the delay of one-month in collections. There is also no guarantee that payment will ever be received. The firm must also consider their product costs which will still be incurred prior to sale. With the delay in collections and potential need for additional inventory the firm must consider its financing costs as its cash cycle will increase. Difficulty: 2 Medium Section: 28.3 Analyzing Credit Policy Topic: Credit policy analysis Bloom's: Apply AACSB: Knowledge Application Accessibility: Keyboard Navigation 26 Copyright © 2019 McGraw-Hill Education. Uptown Markets recently did an analysis of its credit policy and considered several different options. Once the analysis was completed and reviewed, the firm adopted the most optimal policy. The president then stated: "Now, that's done. So we don't ever have to go through that process again." Do you agree? Justify your answer. Answer: You should not agree. The optimal credit policy and the NPV of any policy are based on variables such as carrying and opportunity costs that can, and do, change over time. Therefore, credit policy analysis should be considered an on-going process rather than a one-time event. Difficulty: 2 Medium Section: 28.3 Analyzing Credit Policy Topic: Credit policy analysis Bloom's: Apply AACSB: Knowledge Application Accessibility: Keyboard Navigation

There are generally considered to be five key factors that should be evaluated when trying to determine if a customer will pay. Write five questions that a credit manager should answer when reviewing a credit application that would address these factors. Answer: Here is one set of sample questions: ∙ Character: Is the customer willing to pay his/her bills? ∙ Capacity: Does the customer have sufficient cash flow to be able to pay his/her bills? ∙ Capital: Does the customer have sufficient financial assets to support this debt? ∙ Collateral: Does the customer have assets, either the item being purchased, or other items, of sufficient value that could be sold to repay this debt? ∙ Conditions: Is the economic and business outlook for the customer's business favorable for the repayment of the customer's debts? Difficulty: 2 Medium Section: 28.5 Credit Analysis Topic: Credit policy analysis Bloom's: Apply AACSB: Knowledge Application Accessibility: Keyboard Navigation 27 Copyright © 2019 McGraw-Hill Education. All rights reserved. No reproduction or distribution without the prior written consent of McGraw-Hill Education.

Explain how inventory is managed under an ABC inventory system. Answer: With an ABC system the items that represent the largest percentage of inventory value are classified as "A" items. Because of their cost, these items are monitored closely and purchased primarily as needed, with minimal, if any, units kept as extra stock. Items of low value are classified as "C" items. These tend to be basic commodity items that are bought in bulk and kept on hand at all times. "B" items fall between these two extremes and are managed accordingly. Difficulty: 2 Medium Section: 28.8 Inventory Management Techniques Topic: ABC inventory management Bloom's: Apply AACSB: Knowledge Application Accessibility: Keyboard Navigation

Explain the purpose of a safety stock and how this relates to reorder points. Answer: Safety stock is the term used for the minimum level of inventory that a firm keeps on hand. This stock is available to the firm should an inventory order be delayed or an unexpected increase in production arise. Reorder points are set at a level such that the ordered inventory should arrive just as the inventory is reduced to its safety stock level. Thus, by determining when inventory will fall to the safety stock level and then backing out the order delivery time, the reorder points can be identified.
